one_line_takeaway: The first robustness study of a drift-adaptive malware detector shows a single fine-tuning framework can defend AdvDA against either white-box PGD (down to 3.2% ASR) or black-box MalGuise (down to 5.1% ASR) but never both -- and that source adversarial training is essential for the former yet ruinously costly for the latter.
key_finding: Undefended AdvDA is completely broken by white-box PGD (100% ASR at both epsilon values, every month) but is only moderately vulnerable to black-box MalGuise (12.9% avg ASR), which the authors attribute to the ResNet-18/DA feature representation attenuating sparse, localized binary edits -- an implicit defense. The framework works, but the winning recipe is threat-specific. Against PGD: source adversarial training is essential -- DART(clean) at epsilon=2/255 leaves 28.6% ASR, while DART(adv)/DART(kl) cut it to 3.2%/3.7% at comparable TPR; the perturbation budget must MATCH the attack (models trained at 2/255 collapse to 75.9-99.9% ASR under an 8/255 attack -- robustness overfits the training radius). Against MalGuise: source adversarial training is counterproductive -- MalGuise(clean) already reaches 5.1% ASR with TPR (70.0%) comparable to undefended AdvDA at 3.2x cost, whereas MalGuise(adv) reaches only marginally-lower 3.2% ASR but crashes clean TPR to 31.3% (a 32-44% drop) at 76.8x training cost. Crucially, robustness does not transfer across threat models: MalGuise-trained models are still 100% bypassed by PGD, and DART-trained models give no MalGuise improvement over the baseline. Robustness is a property of the model-threat PAIRING, not the model. Deployment recommendations: DART(kl) for PGD (best TPR at comparable robustness/cost), MalGuise(clean) for MalGuise; and a future multi-view ensemble (one detector per perturbation family, flag-if-any) to cover both.
core_contribution: The first study of adversarial robustness for a drift-adaptive malware detector. The paper takes AdvDA (prior work, ref [20]) -- a Windows-malware detector that fights concept drift via adversarial domain adaptation (DA), aligning an older labeled source domain with a newer, few-labeled target domain -- and asks whether it can be made robust to evasion attacks. Its own contribution is a 'universal robustification framework' that warm-starts from a pretrained, already domain-adapted AdvDA model and fine-tunes it on adversarially transformed source/target inputs, agnostic to both the attack type and the specific input transformation (Eq. 3, Algorithm 1). The framework is derived from DART (prior work, ref [33], a vision-domain robust-DA method), but the authors generalize it to remove DART's reliance on pseudo-labels (AdvDA already has a few real target labels) and to parameterize the source/target transformations. They instantiate it with five defense variants across two threat models and, through a large empirical study, establish two findings that are the real intellectual payload: (i) source-domain adversarial training is essential for PGD defenses but counterproductive for MalGuise defenses, and (ii) robustness does NOT transfer across the two threat models. NOTE: AdvDA, DART, PGD and the MalGuise attack are all cited prior work; the paper's own novelty is the robustification framework, the fair-comparison protocol, and the empirical characterization -- not those building blocks.
threat_model: Two adversaries sharing one goal: make the malware classifier label malware as benign. (1) White-box PGD attacker: full access to model architecture, parameters, and gradients; cannot alter the model, only the classifier input; applies bounded L-infinity perturbations (epsilon=2/255 and 8/255) in the input feature space via projected gradient descent. Chosen because it is the standard attack all existing robust-DA defenses assume. (2) Black-box MalGuise attacker: no knowledge of training data, algorithm, architecture, or weights; knows only the feature type and can query the deployed detector for a confidence score; restricted to functionality-preserving modifications of real Windows PE binaries (control-flow-graph node/edge mutations via Monte Carlo Tree Search that redirect CALL sites through JMPs into an appended section with semantic NOPs). This is the realistic problem-space threat. The paper stresses that these two induce fundamentally different perturbation geometries (dense norm-bounded pixel perturbations vs. sparse structure-preserving binary edits).
limitations: Scope is Windows PE malware, one detector family (AdvDA), one image-based representation, and exactly two attacks; the authors themselves note robustness is 'model-threat pairing' specific, so conclusions may not generalize to other detectors/feature spaces. Only two PGD budgets and one MalGuise configuration (6 levels x 40 iters, 5% size cap) are tested -- larger MCTS budgets are deemed impractical but not evaluated, leaving open whether a better-resourced black-box attacker breaks the low MalGuise ASR. The proposed multi-view ensemble defense is only sketched as future work, not built or evaluated; its FPR and cost impact are unassessed. The 'surprising' baseline MalGuise robustness rests on the specific ResNet-18 backbone and could vanish with a different architecture (indeed the authors note MalConv, a raw-byte 1D-CNN, is >90% bypassed by MalGuise). ASR is measured only on the conservative common malware set (most-confidently-detected samples), which may understate real-world attackability on borderline malware. Benign samples are treated as temporally stable (no timestamps), so drift is modeled only on the malware side.

datasets_used: MB-24+ (ref [19]), an extended variant of MB-24 (ref [20]): nine months of Windows malware March-December 2024 from the MalwareBazaar daily feed, deduplicated by SHA-256
Malware monthly counts 1,080-1,618 samples; 81-126 families/month; 27%-50% of families each month are new relative to the prior month (Table 1)
16,000 benign Windows PE files (clean Windows 8/10/11 installs + common apps)
Source (pre-drift) = Mar-May 2024 split 75/25; target (post-drift) = Jul-Dec 2024; June deliberately omitted to create a temporal gap
Monthly adaptation: fixed source-train set + 500 randomly sampled target instances from month t, evaluated on month t+1, giving five windows (Jul->Aug ... Nov->Dec)
quantitative_results: Undefended AdvDA: PGD ASR 100% at both epsilon=2/255 and 8/255; MalGuise ASR 12.9%+/-8.8; clean TPR 70.3%; cost 0.24h (Table 4)
DART(clean) epsilon=2/255: PGD-2 ASR 28.6%, PGD-8 ASR 99.9%, MalGuise ASR 15.0%, TPR 68.4%, cost 0.71h (3.0x)
DART(adv) epsilon=2/255: PGD-2 ASR 3.2%, PGD-8 ASR 75.9%, MalGuise 13.8%, TPR 66.6%, cost 0.90h (3.8x)
DART(kl) epsilon=2/255: PGD-2 ASR 3.7%, PGD-8 ASR 86.7%, MalGuise 11.6%, TPR 69.0%, cost 1.08h (4.5x)
DART(adv) epsilon=8/255: PGD-2 ASR 1.2%, PGD-8 ASR 12.1%, MalGuise 10.2%, TPR 57.5% (matched-budget robustness, moderate TPR drop)
DART(kl) epsilon=8/255: PGD-2 ASR 1.6%, PGD-8 ASR 15.6%, TPR 60.1%
MalGuise(clean): MalGuise ASR 5.1%+/-3.1, clean TPR 70.0%, PGD 100%/100%, cost 0.76h (3.2x)
MalGuise(adv): MalGuise ASR 3.2%+/-2.7 (best), but clean TPR crashes to 31.3% (32-44% drop) and cost 18.40h (76.8x, of which 17.66h is source-binary attack generation)
Cross-attack: MalGuise variants 100% bypassed by PGD; DART variants give no consistent MalGuise improvement over AdvDA (Figure 4)
Sanity check: reproduced MalGuise's reported >90% ASR on their MalConv model at 1% FPR, confirming attack fidelity
baselines_compared: AdvDA (ref [20]) with no defense -- primary baseline detector
DART (ref [33]) instantiated as three source variants (clean/adv/kl) x two PGD budgets = 6 DART models
MalGuise-based adversarial training, two source variants (clean/adv)
Attacks used as adversaries: PGD (ref [27], white-box, feature-space) and MalGuise (ref [21], black-box MCTS binary evasion)
Implicit comparison of source-transformation choices (clean vs adv vs kl) to isolate source-domain robustness transferability (RQ5)
